coredata.dc:title: Enhancing corrosion protection in acidic environments through biomass-derived carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Li
Zheng, Siyuan
Ma, Xuemei
Zhu, Hailin
Hu, Zhiyong
Sun, Yongqiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an effective and eco-friendly biomass carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) inhibitors were prepared by hydrothermal method from pitaya peels. Physicochemical characterization revealed that the particle size and carbon content of BCQD changed depending on the preparation time. In hydrochloric acid solution, BCQDs with smaller particle-sized and/or higher carbon content performed a stronger corrosion inhibition effect according to electrochemical tests. An inhibition efficiency of over 95% could be achieved. Additionally, the inhibition mechanism of BCQDs was also inferred and analyzed. It is expected that this systemic study will provide guidance for the design of nanomaterial corrosion inhibitors in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon quantum dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Inhibition mechanism
Q235 steel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302124337 , 202103021224207 ), the Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2022-137 ) and Open Project Program of the Shanxi Key Laboratory of Advanced Carbon Materials (No. 2022C80305 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization result of pitaya peel BCQDs including FTIR (a); XRD (b); UV (c); FS (d).
TEM results of pitaya peel BCQDs, BCQDs-1 (a); BCQDs-2 (b); BCQDs-3 (c) and BCQDs-4 (d).
The survey XPS spectrum (a) (BCQDs-1, BCQDs-2, BCQDs-3, BCQDs-4) and high-resolution XPS spectrum results of BCQDs-4 (C1s (b), O1s (c) and N1s (d)).
The Nyquist plots with different concentrations of BCQDs at 298 K, BCQDs-1 (a); BCQDs-2 (b); BCQDs-3 (c) and BCQDs-4 (d).
The Bode plots with different concentrations of BCQDs at 298 K, BCQDs-1 (a); BCQDs-2 (b); BCQDs-3 (c) and BCQDs-4 (d).
The equivalent circuit fitting the EIS data.
Tafel curves of Q235 steel in various test solutions, BCQDs-1 (a); BCQDs-2 (b); BCQDs-3 (c) and BCQDs-4 (d).

SEM corrosion morphology and element mapping patterns of Q235 steel in various solutions; (a) blank; (b) BCQDs-1–200 mg/L; (c) BCQDs-2–200 mg/L; (d) BCQDs-3–200 mg/L; (e) BCQDs-4–200 mg/L; (f) mapping of O, Cl and Fe for blank; (g) mapping of O, Cl and Fe for BCQDs-3–200 mg/L.
Contact angle pictures of Q235 carbon steel after immersing under different conditions.
Corrosion inhibition mechanism of BCQDs for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.
The atomic percentages of four BCQDs from survey XPS spectrum.
The parameters for EIS measurements with different concentrations of BCQDs at 298 K.
Corrosion parameters of Q235 steel in various test solutions at 298 K.

head.ce:title: Enhancing corrosion protection in acidic environments through biomass-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dezhou Graduate School, North University of China, Dezhou, Shandong 253034, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dezhou Graduate School, North University of China, Dezhou, Shandong 253034, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an effective and eco-friendly biomass carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) inhibitors were prepared by hydrothermal method from pitaya peels. Physicochemical characterization revealed that the particle size and carbon content of BCQD changed depending on the preparation time. In hydrochloric acid solution, BCQDs with smaller particle-sized and/or higher carbon content performed a stronger corrosion inhibition effect according to electrochemical tests. An inhibition efficiency of over 95% could be achieved. Additionally, the inhibition mechanism of BCQDs was also inferred and analyzed. It is expected that this systemic study will provide guidance for the design of nanomaterial corrosion inhibitors in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of its excellent comprehensive performance and low cost, Q235 steel is widely utilized in engineering structures such as buildings, high-voltage towers, ships, vehicles, and more [1–4]. Before using, pickling is executed to eliminate rust from the metal surface to enhance the smoothness of the steel surface and extend its usage time [5]. Nonetheless, Q235 steel is vulnerable to severe corrosion, making it tricky to regulate during the pickling process. One of the efficient and straightforward methods to prevent Q235 steel from corroding during the pickling process is by adding corrosion inhibitors [6–10].

Given the rising environmental issues and the increasing need for environmental protection, the presently reported organic corrosion inhibitors - such as imidazoline derivatives [11], Schiff bases [12], and quaternary ammonium salts [13] - are eliminated gradually. Therefore, the researchers put their focus on natural extracts, which are considered as green corrosion inhibitors [14]. For example, O.O. Ogunleye et al [15] made a study on the ethanolic extract of loofah leaves and found that the corrosion inhibition efficiency reached 87.89 %, when the concentration reached 1 g/L. While plant extracts have shown some advancements in corrosion inhibition, achieving the desired level of efficiency at low concentrations remains a challenge. Moreover, the intricate composition of plants makes it challenging to extract and separate their extracts, and the exact components that can effectively inhibit corrosion are yet to be identified. Additionally, the extraction and separation process requires a significant amount of organic solvents, which poses environmental challenges and concerns [16].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), being a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, exhibit remarkable characteristics such as photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, low cytotoxicity, and simple synthesis methods. These desirable properties have captured the attention of researchers in a variety of fields [17–20]. In recent years, a fraction of researchers has used CQDs as high-efficiency metal corrosion inhibitors. In a study conducted by Cui et al. [21], N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were synthesized and their efficacy as a corrosion inhibitor on carbon steel in a 1 M hydrochloric acid solution was investigated. Their study, for the first time, demonstrated the significant corrosion inhibition efficiency of N-CQDs. Ye et al. [22] synthesized N-CQDs by incorporating histidine into citric acid precursors and demonstrated that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of N-CQDs was considerably higher than that of histidine or citric acid alone. These findings further support the potential of N-CQDs as a highly effective corrosion inhibitor. Cen et al. [23] prepared nitrogen and sulfur doped CQDs corrosion inhibitors using amino salicylic acid and thiourea as precursors, which showed high corrosion inhibition efficiency at low concentrations. This indicated that the doping of N and S elements could significantly enhance the fluorescence performance, quantum efficiency and corrosion inhibition efficiency of CQDs. The studies mentioned above suggest that altering the structure of CQDs can have a significant impact on their corrosion inhibition efficiency. This highlights the controllable synthesis of CQDs with desired structures is of significant importance for the future development of corrosion inhibitors.

The promising potential of CQDs as corrosion inhibitors, despite there have been relatively few studies on controlling the influence of reaction conditions on the structure of CQDs and their composition and corrosion inhibition properties. It should be noted that even though the CQDs synthesized in the previously mentioned studies exhibit environmentally friendly properties, their preparation still involves the utilization of chemical reagents, which could hinder their overall environmental sustainability. It is crucial to develop new and sustainable preparation methods for CQDs to minimize the use of toxic chemicals and reduce the environmental impact of the synthesis process. Moreover, the distinction between the corrosion inhibition mechanisms of CQDs and conventional organic corrosion inhibitors remains unclear, and there is a need to further investigate the effect of CQD composition on their corrosion inhibition efficacy [24].

In consideration of these issues, this research was conducted to prepare biomass carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) using pitaya peels as the precursor through a hydrothermal reaction. The utilization of pitaya peel is especially advantageous due to its many excellent characteristics, such as being widely available, non-toxic, reasonably priced, good biocompatibility and abundant in both carbon and hetero atoms. Additionally, this study showed sustainable and eco-friendly approach to the preparation of BCQDs, as no chemical reagents were employed during the entire synthesis process. This not only offers an effective solution for metal corrosion, but also facilitates waste reduction and resource recovery through the utilization of renewable raw materials, thereby contributing to a greener and more sustainable environment. Furthermore, the impact of preparation time on the morphology and composition of BCQDs were investigated by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS. The effect of different BCQDs on the corrosion inhibition of Q235 steel in 1 M hydrochloric acid was also explored through the electrochemistry and surface analysis (SEM and AFM) methods. The inhibition mechanism was further analyzed and discussed. The systematic and in-depth insights contribute to the development of new and effective methods for mitigating metal corrosion while promoting the potential applications of BCQDs as corrosion inhibitors.

Analytical grade hydrochloric acid (36 % ∼ 38 %) was obtained from Chengdu Kolon Reagent Factory, while analytical grade anhydrous ethanol (99.7 %) was procured from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. The Q235 steel, consisting of the following chemical elements (wt. %): C ≤ 0.22, Mn ≤ 1.4, Si ≤ 0.35, S ≤ 0.050, P ≤ 0.045, and Fe for balance, was utilized as the metal samples in the experiments. Before using, the Q235 steel samples were cut into small pieces of 0.1 × 0.1 × 0.1 cm for SEM and 1.0 × 1.0 × 0.1 cm for contact angle test. The electrochemical test utilized Q235 steel as the working electrode, which was embedded in epoxy resin and had only one surface (1.0 cm2) exposed to the corrosion solution. In addition, various concentrations of BCQDs inhibitors were added to a 1 M HCl solution to prepare the corrosion solution. A blank control group was established using 1 M HCl solution.

The process for preparing pitaya peel BCQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . To start, the pitaya peel was thoroughly washed with distilled water and left to dry in the open air at a temperature of 45 °C until it reached a constant weight. Afterward, 2 g of finely ground pitaya peel powder was dispersed in 60 ml of deionized water through ultrasonic oscillation for 15 min. Next, the mixture was moved to a hydrothermal reactor made of polytetrafluoroethylene, where it underwent a hydrothermal reaction for 3 h, 5 h, 10 h, and 12 h at a temperature of 180 °C. Upon completion of the reaction, any impurities were removed by utilizing a high-speed centrifuge operating at 8000 rpm/min for 10 min. Afterwards, the supernatant was carefully inserted into a dialysis bag (Adamas-beta®) with a molecular cut off of 1000, and left to dialyze for 48 h. Once the process was completed, the dialysis solution was concentrated via rotary evaporation before being dried at a vacuum temperature of 45 °C for 24 h. This resulted in the creation of pitaya peel BCQDs in powder form. BCQDs inhibitors obtained at different hydrothermal times were labeled as BCQDs-1 (3 h), BCQDs-2 (5 h), BCQDs-3 (10 h) and BCQDs-4 (12 h) respectively.

The optical properties of BCQDs were analyzed using Ultraviolet–Visible absorption spectra (UV) and fluorescence spectra (FS). The absorber of UV was a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 instrument while fluorescence spectra (FS) were acquired using a Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer F-2700 with an emission peak of 320 nm. Additionally, the structural composition of four types of BCQDs was examined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, American Nicolet iS50), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi). The FT-IR analysis had a resolution of 2 cm−1 and covered a wave number range of 500–4000 cm−1. Furthermore, crystal forms of the four BCQDs were determined using the Bruker-AXS/D8 ECO instrument of the German Bruker Company through X-ray diffraction (XRD). The test was conducted over an angle range of 5°-75° at a sweep speed of 5°/min and step length of 0.02°. And X-ray source of XPS test was AlKα (1486.6 eV), and the incident angle was 90°. For XPS data, the Avantage software was used for calibration and peak splitting. All binding energy could be corrected with C1s (284.8 eV) peak. In addition, the BCQDs’ morphology was examined using a Talos F200S Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) from ThermoFisher Scientific.

The electrochemical experiments were conducted using a Shanghai Chenhua CHI 760E electrochemical workstation at a temperature of 298 K. The traditional three-electrode system was utilized for testing, consisting of a working electrode made of Q235 steel, an auxiliary electrode made of platinum, and a reference electrode made of saturated calomel. Before the test, the work surface of the work electrode underwent a series of polishing steps using emery paper with varying grits of #400, #800, #1500, and #3000 until it reached a mirror-like surface. Afterward, the electrode was subjected to ultrasonic cleaning with absolute ethyl alcohol and dried at room temperature. The open circuit potential (OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and potentiodynamic polarization curves were tested sequentially. The OCP was first tested for 1200 s until reaching a steady state. Subsequently, the EIS was recorded with a 5 mV disturbance signal over a frequency range of 10-2 to 105 Hz. The EIS results were then analyzed using Zsimpwin software. Finally, the potentiodynamic polarization curves were performed at a scan rate of 1 mV/s. The polarization range was set at ±250 mV, based on the OCP. The corrosion inhibition efficiency formula was shown as follows (1–2): [25,26]
(1)
η
( % )
=
R
ct
-
R
ct , 0
R
ct
× 100 %
(2)
η
( % )
=
i
corr , 0
-
i
corr
i
corr , 0
× 100 %

The values of i corr,0 and R ct,0 denote the corrosion current density and charge transfer resistance of the blank solution, while i corr and R ct represent these same values for solutions with varying concentrations of BCQDs.

In the surface morphology experiments, a Japan Fe-SEM, JEOL-JSM-7800F field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and contact angle meter (Kruss DSA25s) were utilized. Prior to the experiment, Q235 steel samples measuring 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm were subjected to sequential polishing using #400, #800, #1500, #3000, #5000, and #7000 polishing grits to achieve a mirror surface. Subsequently, the samples were cleaned using ethanol and ultrapure water ultrasonic treatment, and dried under vacuum.

The procedure involved immersing the samples separately in both a 1 M hydrochloric acid solution with and without 200 mg/L of BCQDs, at a temperature of 298 K, for a duration of 12 h. After immersing, the samples were taken out, washed with ultrapure water, and left to dry at room temperature for subsequent usage.
To investigate the impact of hydrothermal time on BCQDs, various characterization techniques including FTIR, UV, PL and XRD were employed to analyze the BCQDs as shown in Fig. 2 .

Based on Fig. 2(a), it is evident that the BCQDs exhibit unique and recognizable peaks associated with specific functional groups. The vibrational peaks observed at different wavenumbers in the infrared spectrum of BCQDs suggest the presence of various functional groups. A peak at 3432 cm−1 indicates N-H stretching vibration, while the broad absorption at 3153 cm−1 can be attributed to O-H stretching vibration in the carboxyl group of BCQDs. A clear and characteristic peak is also detected at 1556–1571 cm−1, which corresponds to the skeleton vibration of C = C in the aromatic ring. Moreover, a specific absorption peak at 1244 cm−1 is pronounced in BCQDs-1, and at 1304 cm−1 in the other three BCQDs, indicating the stretching absorption of the C-O bond in the carboxylic acid. The peak at approximately 1042 cm−1 is believed to be associated with the vibrational modes of SO, OCO, –OH (alcoholic hydroxyl group), or/and C-O, while the peak at 832 cm−1 represents the out-of-plane bending vibration of C-H in aromatics [27–29]. Based on the FTIR spectroscopy analysis, it can be concluded that the prepared BCQDs possess a high concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups, including –OH and –COOH. Besides, the functional group types of all BCQDs are nearly same. However, there are some differences in the relative intensity of the peaks, which indicate that different reaction time has some effect on the content of functional groups, resulting the different structures of BCQDs. The abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups like –OH and –COOH in the BCQDs may have a significant impact on the corrosion inhibition efficiency.

Fig. 2(b) illustrates a comparison of X-ray diffraction patterns between BCQDs and the precursor from pitaya peel. Comparing the obtained BCQDs with the precursor, BCQDs showed a diffraction peak at 2θ about 18.03°.The shape is found to be consistent with the diffraction peak of amorphous carbon by PDF card comparison, which indicates that BCQDs have a graphene-like structure [30]. As for the sharp peaks at 2θ of 28.67° and 40.69°, it is determined by PDF card comparison that they are the crystalline forms corresponding to KCl and NaCl, respectively, due to the precursors being biomass containing some inorganic salts.

In Fig. 2(c), two absorption peaks can be observed in the UV spectrum at 250 nm and 320 nm respectively for all BCQDs. These peaks may be attributed to the π → π* electron transition of CC bonds or/and the n → π* electron transition of CO bonds in BCQDs [31,32]. As the hydrothermal reaction time increases (3 h, 5 h, 10 h and 12 h), the absorption peak intensity noticeably increases, indicating an increase in the number of unsaturated bonds in BCQDs. This suggests that the content of unsaturated bonds increases with extended hydrothermal reaction time. Based on literature research, electron-rich groups, such as heteroatomic groups containing N, O, and S, as well as unsaturated conjugated systems, have the ability to donate electrons to the vacant orbitals of metals that are adsorbed on metal surfaces. This offers significant benefits for metal corrosion prevention [33,34].

The FS in Fig. 2(d) show the emission spectrum with the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, which has an emission peak at 392 nm [35,36], indicating that all four BCQDs synthesized in this study have excellent fluorescence. Notably, the peak intensity of BCQDs is variable, underscoring the considerable impact of hydrothermal duration on the fluorescence properties of BCQDs.

The TEM images show in Fig. 3 explore the morphology and particle size of BCQDs prepared for various prepared time. Obviously, the morphology of all BCQDs are all uniform spherical carbon structures with the particle sizes of 3–6 nm. Meanwhile, the interplanar spacing measured by TEM is about 0.21 nm, they all conform to the graphene lattice interplanar spacing [37,38]. Upon analyzing the particle size distribution, it becomes apparent that the average BCQD size exhibits a decreasing trend as the reaction time increases. The above reasons are owing to the fact that the reaction time in the hydrothermal reaction has a certain degree of influence on the nanomaterial morphology. It is evident that the particle size reaches its minimum values after 12 h, with an average particle size of 3 nm.

The XPS technique, which is a crucial method for surface analysis, was employed to conduct a more comprehensive investigation of the chemical state and elemental composition of BCQDs at different hydrothermal durations. Fig. 4 illustrates the survey, C1s, O1s, and N1s high-resolution XPS spectra, while Table 1 lists the corresponding atomic percentages.

Based on the XPS spectra collected in survey (Fig. 4(a)), it can be concluded that the four BCQDs primarily consist of elements such as C, O, N, Cl, P, and K. Because the precursors of BCQDs synthesized in this article were pitaya peels, their element composition is more complex than that of BCQDs synthesized by chemical reagents. It is obvious that the most elements in BCQDs are C and O, and the ratio of C to O increases with reaction time. The elemental content percentage reveals that the percentage of element C escalates as the hydrothermal time increases, indicating that the carbonization of BCQDs is reliant on the reaction time. The carbonization of BCQDs has a direct impact on the stability of the adsorption layer formed on the surface of the carbon steel. A higher carbonization rate may result in a more hydrophobic adsorption layer, which could enhance the hydrophobicity of the metal surface and effectively isolate the contact between the carbon steel and the corrosive medium.

In Fig. 4(b), the spectrum of C1s shows a peak position at 284.82 eV which corresponds to sp2 orbital hybridized of CC, and another peak position at 286.37 eV that corresponds to sp3 orbital hybridized of C-O. A third peak at 287.98 eV indicates OCO [39,40]. In Fig. 4(c), the high resolution of N1s shows three peaks in the peak fitting. The peaks at 398.71 eV, 399.87 eV and 401.85 eV correspond to NH, NC and NC, respectively [41]. Finally, in the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 4(d)), the peak at 531.93 eV represents CO, while the peak at 532.32 eV corresponds to CO [42]. Based on XPS reports, the BCQDs possess a copious amount of elements such as C, O, and N.

Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 present the EIS plots of Q235 steel electrode in 1 M HCl, with and without varying concentrations of BCQDs, at 298 K. In Fig. 7 , the equivalent circuit utilized for fitting the EIS data is depicted, consisting of Rs (representing the solution resistance), R ct (representing the charge transfer resistance), and CPE (represented by the formula for constant phase angle element) (3) [43]:
(3)
Z
CPE
=
1
Y 0
jw
n

Here, j represents the imaginary root, ω represents the angular frequency, and n represents the dispersion effect index. The latter factor characterizes the roughness and unevenness of the working electrode surface. The value range of n is between −1 and 1, and when n is close to 1, CPE is considered a capacitor. The capacitance value of C dl can be calculated by the following formula (4) [44]:
(4)
C
dl
=
Y 0
w
n - 1
=
Y 0
2 π
f
Zim - M a x
n - 1

Based on the Nyquist plots, it is evident that the impedance diagram consists of a capacitive loop. As the concentration of BCQDs increases, the radius of the capacitive loop tends to increase, and the charge transfer resistance shows an increasing trend. This indicates that the corrosion control process is primarily driven by the process of charge transfer. In addition, the shape of the capacitive reactance arc remained unchanged, suggesting that the corrosion inhibition mechanism remained consistent with the increase of BCQDs concentration [45]. The results are once again proved by the Bode diagram. With an increase in BCQDs concentration, both the impedance modulus and phase angle showed a corresponding increase. The optimal corrosion inhibition efficiency was achieved at a concentration of 200 mg/L.

The EIS data in Table 2 demonstrates that as the concentration of BCQDs increases, there is a corresponding increase in the value of charge transfer resistance (R ct). In addition, both the electrical double layer (C dl) values and the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) values show gradual and opposite trends - C dl decreasing and η increasing. The interface electric double layer is explained by the following equation [46] (5):
(5)
C
d l
=
ε
0
ε
d
S

In the given formula, the ε 0 represents the dielectric constant of air whereas ε represents the local dielectric constant of the electric double layer. The variable S represents the area of the electrode that is exposed to air while the variable d represents the thickness of the electric double layer. Together, these variables describe the behavior and characteristics of the electric double layer, which plays a vital role in electrochemical reactions. At the optimal concentration, the inhibition efficiency values are 94.4 % (BCQDs-1), 94.6 % (BCQDs-2), 95.6 %(BCQDs-3) and 94.9 % (BCQDs-4), which implies better corrosion inhibition efficiencies at high concentrations with a preparation time of 10 h (BCQDs-3). Combined with TEM and XPS analysis, it is found that BCQDs with smaller average particle size show more obvious corrosion inhibition effect in electrochemical test [47]. The BCQDs with smaller particle size can form more dense covering layer on the surface of Q235 steel compared with BCQDs with large particle size, so that it can better isolate the contact between corrosive media and carbon steel [48]. The main ways in which BCQDs form protective films on carbon steel surfaces are agglomerative sedimentation and adsorption. At high concentrations, the settlement and reunion behavior of BCQDs forming a film with the metal surface is more significant than the adsorption behavior. However, the agglomeration phenomenon is suppressed as the concentration decreases, and the film formation is primarily achieved through adsorption, exhibiting slightly lower corrosion inhibition efficiency at low concentrations.

Fig. 8 displays the potentiodynamic polarization curves of the Q235 electrode in 1 M hydrochloric acid solution with varying concentrations of the four BCQDs. Table 3 presents the data obtained through the linear extrapolation method. The Fig. 8 results show that the addition of BCQDs can effectively impede the corrosion of Q235 steel in 1 M hydrochloric acid solution. The corrosion current density of BCQDs is considerably lower than that of the blank. An increase in BCQDs concentration results in a decrease in the corrosion current density. Both the cathodic and anodic branch of the corrosion current density are diminished, indicating that BCQDs can inhibit the cathode and anode reactions of Q235 steel.

In Table 3, the corrosion potential (E corr) moves towards a more negative direction, while the corrosion current density (i corr) gradually decreases as the BCQDs concentration increases. Based on the Tafel data, it can be observed that the corrosion potential experiences a negative shift with a change value that is below 85 mV. This indicates that the BCQDs synthesized possess characteristics of a mixed-type inhibitor [49,50]. The optimal corrosion inhibition efficiency is achieved at a BCQDs concentration of 200 mg/L, exhibiting values of 95.5 % (BCQDS-4), 96.1 % (BCQDS-3), 95.3 % (BCQDS-2), and 94.7 % (BCQDS-1), respectively. These results demonstrate that the corrosion inhibition efficiency reaches the highest for preparation time of 10 h, which corresponds with the EIS findings.

Fig. 9 illustrates the SEM images and element mapping patterns of Q235 steel after immersion in an HCl solution, both with and without four different types of BCQDs. Upon observation, it becomes apparent that the Q235 steel surface subjected to immersion in the blank solution exhibits the most severe corrosion, with the corrosion marks being particularly evident. And the surface condition of the samples processed with BCQDs has been significantly improved, its surface is relatively smooth. At high magnification, it is not difficult to see that there are many adsorbents on the surface of the BCQDs sample. In addition, the uniform distribution of all elements on the surface of the blank sample (Fig. 9(f)) indicates that the corrosion of Q235 steel is uniform. It is evident that the Q235 steel surface with BCQDs (Fig. 9(g)) exhibits an enrichment of oxygen (O) and chlorine (Cl) elements. However, the distribution of iron (Fe) elements remains uniform. Through the previous XPS and XRD analysis of BCQDs, the content of O elements of BCQDs is high proportion, and Cl element is also detected. The results demonstrated that the enrichment area of O element and Cl element may be BCQDs adsorbed on the surface of Q235 steel. The surface of the sample is socked in HCl solution with BCQDs-3 has the least holes caused by corrosion. Based on the comparable corrosion inhibition efficiency of BCQDs-3 and BCQDs-4, this study has selected a reaction time of 10 h as the optimal choice, taking into account energy consumption considerations.

Fig. 10 displays the contact angle images of different samples: the blank sample, and samples treated with BCQDs-1, BCQDs-2, BCQDs-3, and BCQDs-4 in a 1 M hydrochloric acid solution for 24 h. The corresponding contact angles observed in the pictures are 35°, 49°, 62°, 70°, and 80°, indicating that the hydrophobicity (water-repellent nature) of the Q235 steel surface increases with the addition of BCQDs. It is observed that the hydrophobicity of the Q235 steel surface treated with BCQDs increases as the hydrothermal time of BCQDs increases. The XPS analysis mentioned earlier shows that the increase in carbon (C) element content in BCQDs enhances the stability of the carbon layer formed on the Q235 steel surface during the corrosion inhibition process. Additionally, BCQDs with smaller average particle size result in a more compact formation layer that better isolates the contact between Q235 steel and the corrosion media. The above findings suggest that the addition of BCQDs can enhance its surface hydrophobicity and improve its resistance to corrosion.

The present findings support that the synthesis of BCQDs from pitaya peel, a biomass precursor, effectively hinders the corrosion of Q235 steel in a 1 M HCl solution. The inhibition mechanism can be illustrated by Fig. 11 . Firstly, BCQDs are nanomaterials with high surface energy, resulting in their sub-aggregation. This property allows them to agglomerate and settle on the metal surface in the corrosive solution, forming a protective layer [51]. In addition, BCQDs contain oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) elements, making them susceptible to chemical adsorption with metal surfaces. The presence of defects on the surface of BCQDs facilitates the accumulation of charge and physical adsorption with the metal [52]. The BCQDs adsorbed on the metal surface can continue to agglomerate with the BCQDs in the corrosive solution. This enhances the driving force to form a more compact and protective film on the metal surface. Overall, the combination of sub-aggregation, chemical adsorption, and continuous agglomeration of BCQDs contributes to the formation and maintenance of a protective film, effectively inhibiting the corrosion process on the Q235 steel surface.
The eco-friendly BCQDs were successfully synthesized by utilizing pitaya peel as a precursor instead of organic reagents. The main conclusions are as follows:
(1) These biomass-based BCQDs exhibitoutstanding corrosion inhibition properties for Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution.
(2) The corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 95.6 % at a concentration of 200 mg/L.
(3) The hydrothermal reaction time has been found to influence the morphology and composition of BCQDs.
(4) The increasing carbon content and reducing particle size enhance corrosion inhibition efficiency.

Li Feng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Siyuan Zheng: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xuemei Ma: Investigation. Hailin Zhu: Resources, Supervision. Zhiyong Hu: Software, Validation. Yongqiang Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302124337, 202103021224207), the Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2022-137) and Open Project Program of the Shanxi Key Laboratory of Advanced Carbon Materials (No. 2022C80305).
